sentences.text: I want to share a poem, which is called 'A Nazi Murderer in Snow White Gloves.' And it was written April 29th, 1982.
The poem was motivated because I remembered a phrase that my family was using.
In Yiddish, they used to say-- and I'll explain it in English.
The [YIDDISH], when somebody pretended to be something that he wasn't, and he was cruel or he was, you know, destructive, but pretended to be a gentleman.
So my-- my father and mother used to use this expression, [YIDDISH].
And it so happened that when I arrived to Auschwitz, Mengele was wearing white gloves.

And when I was looking at the Nazis in contrast, how they looked nice and clean and-- and well-fed, and we were starved and looked filthy and horrible-- so in a way, it-- it made it for them easier to do to them what they did, because they didn't see us.
They tried to view us as subhuman, as vermin.
So vermin, you're a bug.
If you don't identify with, it's easier to kill, you know.
He's a-- he is a Nazi murderer in snow white gloves, a university educated man with two doctoral degrees.
He's the infamous Dr.
Josef Mengele, Birkenau's angel of death.
He knows how to heal the sick, but perversely chooses to kill healthy Jews enslaved in the killing center of Auschwitz.
More than 400,000 innocent men, women, and little children died at his hands.
And among them, unfortunately, my entire family of blessed memory was murdered on November 22nd, 1942 in the gas chambers of Auschwitz.'
Yes.
I saw Mengele on numerous occasions.
Because they used to do [NON-ENGLISH].
OK?
They also used to do selections.

[NON-ENGLISH] means since we didn't get a change of clothes, we were wearing constantly the same clothes, so they would take-- take these clothes and put them to get the-- what calls?-- disinfect them.
OK?
But also, he came to make selections.
He selected, you know, who should go to the gas chambers many times.
He selected people for experiments.
When Mengele arrived, we all died inside by inches.
Oh, yes.
By that time, I-- we knew who he was, the beginning.
And this is what troubled me much more, because if they were barbarians, but they were educated people.
And this is why I started to question everything.
I said, so what is education if it leads to Auschwitz?
What is religion?
People who are religious-- people-- Mengele from what I read about him later came from a fine family, was well educated, had degree.

In fact, nowadays if somebody gives me an order, I know right away, this is not a person I want to associate, because the first sign of respect is when you want something-- I tell them, I honor requests.
I don't take orders, only from God, you know,
The poem is called 'The Silent Voice,' and it was written May 11, 1980. 'Dedicated to all those whose voices have been silenced forever in the Holocaust from 1933 'til 1945.
When ages of civilization crumble to dust and death is raging supreme, when the devil is crowned by the power of deceit, then freedom is a faraway thing.
When all that you have loved is taken from you and you are stripped to the bare bone, when all moral boundaries and laws are broken, then you are in hell with Satan alone.
When Jews are depicted as subhuman, as vermin, and brute force and evil prevail, when doctors and nurses turn children to corpses, then all logic and reason fail.
When the whole world remains dead silent, with the exception of just a few, then they grant Satan license and opportunity to murder in the gas chambers a God-loving Jew.

When all heads are shaved, all human rights denied, and all wear the same kind of tattered striped dress, when to worship-- the worship of God becomes a crime, then the whole world is in a terrible mess.
When Hitler's dream becomes a reality and the Nazis send Jews to the left and to the right, then such men are not ruled by God's divine power, but by their own burning ambition for power and might.
When this so-called superior Aryan master race used all their technology, knowledge, and skill to murder six million Jews merely for being born Jewish, that was not God's, but Hitler's demonic will.'
Hope never.
Hope never.
Even in the time when I was praying for-- for death, I didn't lose hope.
It's just that I-- I couldn't-- I didn't know how to deal with the pain.
I don't think I really wanted to die.
I just probably prayed for a relief of the pain.
I didn't know how to express it.
Because the very fact that-- once I found out that they want us dead, I did everything to survive.

I tried to keep myself as clean as I could, because I know that made a big difference, as healthy as I could, mental attitude, and above all, not to give up hope, because my parents and my grandmother used to say, [NON-ENGLISH].
As long one vein is still-- as long the heart is beating, there is always hope.
Another expression that my father used a lot, [NON-ENGLISH].
A minute before 12:00 is not 12:00.
And I use it now.
A fact is not a fact 'til after the fact.
You know in Jewish, from the [NON-ENGLISH], you don't talk-- you don't make-- you can dream about the future.
You can plan of the future.
But you don't make a commitment about something you're going to do.
For all you know, you might not be alive.
And it's there, where we were dying, that I learned the value of life and to live for now, and most of all, to be positive.
And I remember distinctly, I was always a very spiritual person.
I realize that now.
I didn't know at the time that's called spirituality.
But I am a very intuitive person.
I trust my feelings and thoughts.
So sometimes, you know, intuition has nothing to do with logic.
So sometimes when I get a strong feeling to do or not to do something, it's not that I know I have a looking glass I can tell you what's going to be the future.
But just the heart tells me [NON-ENGLISH].
There is a saying, 'The heart knows reason that reason doesn't know of.'
About Auschwitz, I remembered constantly people dying.
I remember everything was mountain.

How there was-- I have some pictures later, I'll show you the mountains of the bodies laying, people they're using-- they had like little wagons-- I forgot what they're called-- to wheel the dead people.

And how people were trying-- how we felt all in the same boat, how we were constantly living in those conditions, and still hoping and still trying to hold onto the things that we believed, and helping each other.
And that's when we were chased out.
We didn't-- I didn't know the point if somebody left.
I found out later that some people were still left, the sick ones and everything.
But we were driven on foot.
We left Auschwitz January 18th, 1945.
I didn't know anything what was going on the outside world.
We were isolated.
We didn't have anything.
No.
No.
Didn't know anything, because-- and then we went-- began the forced six-day death march.
And Bina and I fortunately were still alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I made connection with other people, you know, with other inmates in Auschwitz.
But she was my dearest friend.
We were like sisters.
Some people said, you don't look like sisters.
They thought we are sisters.
But we were spiritual sisters, and we are 'til this day.
And we started on the death march without food, without water, chased.
All the-- that's when I saw people shot down.
I didn't see so much shootings in Auschwitz per se.
Was I beaten in Auschwitz?
Actually, no.

I tried for the most part-- even though I was starved, I didn't want to put in the-- my bowl in the [INAUDIBLE], because I knew that if I'm going to get hit, it's going to hurt more than the [INAUDIBLE].
And fortunately, I was able to escape without beating.
OK, you're shoved and pushed like this here, and called all kinds of name, and, you know, abused.
They used to call us, excuse my expression, [NON-ENGLISH], which means what people do in the toilet, you know.
I don't know the--
Excrement.
Yes.
You know, to try to dehumanize us, to make us feel worthless.
And this is what I always remembered to fight against it.
And the only way I could fight is not to say-- I thought to myself-- it sounds funny.
If they would tell me I'm a chair, I wouldn't believe them.
Why should I believe them that I'm a number?
Numbers cannot think.
They cannot feel.
They cannot act.
They cannot remember.
And I do.
And I have a body, and a soul, and a mind.
They can enslave my-- my body, but they cannot enslave my mind and soul.
So this is how I-- and this kept-- what kept me going is the hope.
And because I knew already what they want to do.
So I said, I'm not going to collaborate in my own destruction.
I'm not going to give them the final victory.
I said, if I die, I have no choice.
Everybody die.
But it's how I'm going to die, that I'm going to determine, what kind of death.
Yes.
The death march was the most horrible thing, because some people who survived were shot on the way.
Anyone who couldn't keep up with the marching, [INAUDIBLE].
If somebody wanted-- fell and you wanted to help, you both got shot.
So therefore, it was terrible torture, because when you see somebody you care, you cannot-- they don't let you to help.
This was the process that they tied to dehumanize us.
And we were holding on, and we were going and-- oddly, it's just-- it's amazing how strong you become when you have to.
You don't even know.

I remember what my mother said when she read [NON-ENGLISH], '[INAUDIBLE] of the Silent.' You really don't know you're strength 'til you're tested, that a person sometimes stronger than iron and sometimes weaker than a fly.
And we were going.
And people were-- some were crying.
Some fell.
Some who survived were shot on the way.
Some were beaten.
And we couldn't do anything about it.
We had-- if we wanted to survive, we had to march on.
And this was the problem.
Well, there were girls shot, yes, who were marching with us, were shot on the way, yes.
And--
Because they couldn't keep up with the guard, because they fell, because they didn't have the strength.
So You saw all the way-- all along the way people on the road shot.
So this is why they call it a death march, so this was the final test.
Either you live or survive.
And I didn't know how long, but-- you know, how long this is going to take.
But finally, when we came closer to the German [INAUDIBLE], they took us on some kind of trucks again.
And we were both to another killing center in Ravensbruck, Germany.
Why I call it a killing center is there were-- we were all brought there to be-- to die, to be destroyed.
But in certain camps, they had-- in certain-- in certain places they had gas chamber and crematorias, and in others, they didn't.
And Ravensbruck was a concentration camp just for women, and they did have.
And by the way, I went back last year, which was in 1955, was invited by the German government.
And I went back to--
Oh, I said 1955.
I made a mistake. 1995, last year.
And when we were there, some-- a woman who was from Poland but not Jewish, told me, you were supposed to be-- she asked me where I'm from.
She saw the number.
She said where I'm from.
And she said to me, I didn't mean it in a bad way.
I meant that there was an order that the people who were-- came from the death march to Auschwitz to finish off-- they didn't want to have any eyewitnesses.
That's why they tried to destroy everything and to-- for nobody to survive.
That was Hitler's plan.
Even when he was losing the war, he still was going on with destroying Jews.
The point is one thing, that this-- it's-- for this, I don't even have an answer.

It was-- you know, I survived against all possible odds, because in Hitler's totalitarian state, mass murder of the Jews was legal, but to save a Jewish child from Nazi destruction was illegal and punishable by death.
Everything that we did was illegal.
To worship God was illegal, but to burn the-- the Jewish synagogue, that was-- that was legal.
So it was a reversal of our commandments.
And-- you know, the Nazis did everything in their power to dehumanize and to destroy me.
And I told him that my family taught me that who I am, my [NON-ENGLISH], my humaneness, does not depend on others-- how others treat me, but how I treat others.
I have not killed anybody in my life.
I have not come committed a crime, yet I have been-- everything that I loved was taken from me and destroyed.
So the [INAUDIBLE]?
Oh, OK.
So we came to Ravensbruck.
In Ravensbruck, we came in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we left January 18 and I think we came February 23rd.
It was not-- it was like a transit camp more.
I didn't stay that long.
And then they send me to-- from there, we were sent to another camp, Bina and I, to Malchow, Germany.
Malchow, fortunately-- it was bad enough too, but-- still the appel, still starvation, still bad conditions.
But the only good thing was there was no longer the fear of gas chambers.
There was no gas chambers in Malchow, Germany, because from what I'm told, Ravensbruck was the only one on German soil that had gas chambers.
And there we stayed 'til Passover, April 26, 1945.
When I was there?
Absolutely not.
Who had a calendar?
Who'd know?
And if I knew, what could I do about it?
I didn't have enough to keep body and soul, you know?
So-- it was-- it was-- you know, it might be a coincidence, but I was born during Passover, April 15, 1926.
I was liberated during Passover, April 26, 1945.
And I read a book, which is called 'The Wasteland' by TS Eliot, when he says that April is the worst month in the year.

Well, [NON-ENGLISH], thank God, for me, it was a good month, because that's when I was born and I was liberated, and I have survived to tell my story, which is not completely yet finished, because after this, we were not completely free yet.
Yes, let me please just finish.
And from-- from our skins, they made lampshades; from our bones, fertilizers; so everything for the German economy.
And this is why I discovered one thing, what is the real value, and what is the meaning of life.
And I said, we cannot live without trust, so we went on.
And we were liberated.
Not everybody was taken there, I don't think.
I don't know whether everybody went there.
But I was sick, and I was taken to-- by the Swedish Red Cross.
There was a girl.
Her name was [INAUDIBLE].
And while we were-- the war was not officially ended.
By the way, Folke Bernadotte, Bernadotte, he was the one, you know, from-- through the Swedish Red Cross, that they liberated us.
No.
No.
I never met Hitler either, yet he influenced my destiny, so I learned people can influence your destiny without being personal knowing you or not knowing you.
So anyhow, so when we were liberated, there was still the war was going on.
And a piece of bomb fell in the-- on our truck.
And unfortunately, my friend [INAUDIBLE] hit her leg.
And she had-- when we came to Sweden, her leg was amputated.
When we came on the Swedish soil.
And I saw the-- the contrast of people here, people the doctors [INAUDIBLE] to kill, and here, people tried to help, to heal.
And I was finally free.
I was alive.
But I had no home to live in.
A hospital bed became my first home.
I had-- I was 19 years old.
I was-- didn't have-- couldn't-- unlike my non-Jewish fellow survivors, I could not go back, because Jewish homes, Jewish families, and Jewish communities in Poland were destroyed.
I was stateless, homeless, penniless, jobless.
I had no marketable skills, only seven grades of public school and a few grades of Hebrew school.
I had no one left to love me, to help me, or to miss me.
The only person in the world that I knew at that time and had was Bina, and Bina had me.
But I was free.
But I had no place to go, so the transition was terrible, and I was sick.
And I will never forget when I saw myself.
Yeah.
Before we entered the hospital, everything was burnt, because it was not clean, [INAUDIBLE].

I had this striped tatters dress, minus my hair, minus my monthly cycle, because in Auschwitz, we didn't get the monthly cycle, which worried me, because I didn't know if I'll ever be able-- know if it's temporary or not.
To be able to have a family.
And we came into Sweden.
The people in the hospital were very nice.
But when I saw myself the first time in the mirror, I became hysterical.
I started to yell.
I couldn't speak the language and the nurses didn't know what's happened.
They thought that I suffered some kind of shock or something.
And I obviously must have, because I didn't know who I was.
I didn't look like a woman at all.
My body was skeletal body, everything.
And only what I have, which I gained, was the number and the experience.
And I was there.
